nativeRange.habitat: Lakes, ponds, and marshes in flat, open grassland and seasonally flooded habitats of northwestern Venezuela and northeastern Colombia, including the Maracaibo Basin and (as an introduced and expanding population) the Lake Valencia Basin. Capable of crossing land between water bodies during rains or drying.
nativeRange.biotope: Lowland Neotropical pond / grassland flood-pool, generally still or sluggish water with soft substrate and submerged vegetation.

decorPreferences: tightly-fitting lid (escape risk is high and usually fatal)
sand or fine smooth gravel substrate
driftwood and broad-leaved or floating plants for cover
sponge filter or gentle filtration with low current
water column of roughly 30–50 cm for natural posture and breeding

diet.notes: Dwarf Surinam Toads have been reported to accept frozen and prepared foods more readily than many of their Pipa relatives. Generally advised staples include frozen bloodworms, frog and tadpole pellets, and chopped earthworms or red wigglers; many specimens will also take granulated foods aimed at ornamental fish. Live foods are not required.

goodWith: other Dwarf Surinam Toads (groups are commonly kept; ~2 frogs per 5 US gallons / ~19 L beyond the starter group)
calm, mid- to upper-water freshwater fish too large to be swallowed
avoidWith: fish or shrimp small enough to fit in the toad's mouth

summary: The Dwarf Surinam Toad (Pipa parva) is a small, fully aquatic frog from northwestern South America, kept in groups and generally willing to accept frozen and prepared foods, making it one of the more approachable members of the Pipa family.
careNotes: The Dwarf Surinam Toad is the smallest commonly traded member of the genus Pipa, with adults reaching only about 2.7–4.4 cm snout-to-vent — a fraction of the size of the related Surinam Toad (Pipa pipa), which reaches 10–17 cm and is an obligate ambush predator. Captive-bred specimens are available through specialist amphibian breeders and select online suppliers, though the species remains a niche offering rather than a commonly stocked item; where captive-bred animals can be sourced, the wild-caught stress and parasite load sometimes seen in other Pipa are generally not a concern. The species is fully aquatic — no land area is required or beneficial — and is typically kept in groups; a 40 L (approximately 10 US gallon) tank is commonly cited as sufficient for two to three individuals, with roughly two additional frogs per 19 L beyond that. Water is generally maintained warm (around 24–28°C) with pH near neutral; soft or blackwater chemistry is not required, and dechlorinated tap water within these ranges is commonly used. A tightly-fitting lid is essential — Pipa species tend to climb and jump persistently, and escapes from an open aquarium are reported to be almost universally fatal. Sand or fine smooth gravel substrate is generally accepted; these toads are visual ambush feeders rather than substrate-gulpers, so impaction risk is considerably lower than in the larger Pipa pipa. Gentle filtration with low current is advised; like other amphibians, the skin is sensitive to chlorine, copper, and medications, so a dechlorinator should be used and water changes kept conservative.
breedingNotes: The Dwarf Surinam Toad has been reported to breed readily in captivity once mature, with females able to produce three or four clutches per year with minimal external stimulation. Like other Pipa, the pair performs an inverting amplexus — a somersaulting embrace — during which fertilised eggs are pressed into the female's dorsal skin, where they are progressively enveloped by a honeycomb of skin pockets. Notably, and unlike the related Surinam Toad (Pipa pipa), the Dwarf Surinam Toad releases free-swimming tadpoles from the mother's back rather than fully-formed froglets; the tadpoles emerge into the water and complete metamorphosis externally, and are commonly reared on freshly-hatched Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp). A water column of around 30–50 cm is generally advised to allow room for the mating behaviour.

primary.name: Josh's Frogs — Sabana Suriname Toad (Pipa parva) Care
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source. Josh's Frogs returned HTTP 403 to direct WebFetch (consistent with prior runs against this domain) but the page contents were retrievable via WebSearch summarisation. Care guide confirmed: adult size ~1–1.75 in (females) / 1–1.5 in (males), captive lifespan up to 7 years, water temperature in the mid-70s°F, gravid females larger and more rotund, diet of bloodworms, frog/tadpole pellets, and red wigglers (notably accepts prepared foods unlike most Pipa).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Sabana Surinam toad
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pipidae, Anura; synonym Protopipa parva Ruthven & Gaige 1923), native range (NW Venezuela, NE Colombia, Maracaibo Basin; introduced in Lake Valencia Basin), adult size (males 28–37 mm, females 27–44 mm), habitat (lakes, ponds, marshes; capable of crossing land), reproductive biology (tadpoles emerge from the mother's back, NOT fully-formed froglets — key differentiator from Pipa pipa), and IUCN status (Least Concern, locally treated as a pest).

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Pipa parva
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry parameters: water temperature 26–28°C, pH ~6.5–7, water depth 30–50 cm seasonally, complete tank lid required (escapes fatal), accepts all kinds of frozen ornamental-fish foods and many specimens take granulates, tadpoles (not froglets) emerge from dorsal skin and feed on plankton (Artemia nauplii in captivity). Aquarium Glaser is a major German specialty importer/breeder of P. parva for the European hobby trade.

additional.name: Gone Froggin — Sabana Surinam Toad (Pipa parva)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (0.86–1.73 in / 27–44 mm), highly aquatic nature (only crosses land when water bodies dry), and range/habitat description. No additional husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Snakes at Sunset — Pipa parva for sale
additional.notes: Confirmed widespread captive-bred trade availability; juveniles typically offered around 1.5–2 inches, described as 'similar to but way smaller than Pipa pipa'. Diet of frozen bloodworms and other aquatic insect foods.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed readily in captivity once sexually mature, with females producing roughly three to four clutches per year. Eggs are embedded into the female's dorsal skin during inverting amplexus; tadpoles (not froglets) emerge from the mother's back and complete development free-swimming in the water column. Tadpoles are commonly reared on freshly-hatched Artemia nauplii. A water column of roughly 30–50 cm is generally advised for the somersaulting mating behaviour.
